title: PM Toolkit — Project Manager: Power Up

steps.title: Welcome to the Lab
steps.instruction:
Welcome! In this hands-on lab you will use GitHub Copilot Chat to tackle real project management tasks: summarizing pull requests, writing better issues, generating sprint reports, identifying risks, and drafting stakeholder communications.

The guide panel will update automatically as you advance through the slides. The glowing edge and arrow will show you where to look.

steps.title: Open Copilot Chat
steps.instruction: We have opened the Copilot Chat panel for you (Ctrl+Alt+I). This is your primary tool for everything in this lab. As a project manager, you will use Chat to translate, summarize, draft, and analyze.

steps.title: Your First PM Prompt
steps.instruction:
Type this prompt into Copilot Chat:

I'm a project manager working with a development team on GitHub. What are the top 5 ways I can use you to be more effective in my role without writing any code?

Review the response. These are the capabilities you will practice throughout this lab.

steps.title: Panels Closed
steps.instruction:
We've closed the sidebar for you.

For future reference:
• Copilot Chat: Ctrl+Shift+I

steps.title: Configure Your Workspace
steps.instruction:
We've opened Settings for you. Type editor.wordWrap in the search bar and set it to on.

As a project manager, you will be reading and writing a lot of text in VS Code. Word wrap makes long markdown lines easier to read. Small workspace tweaks like this add up to a more comfortable working environment.

steps.title: Plan Custom Fields for a Project Board
steps.instruction:
Ask Copilot to help you design your project board. Type:

I'm setting up a GitHub Project board for a 6-person dev team delivering a web application. Recommend custom fields I should create for sprint planning, including field type and example values for each.

Review the recommendations. Note how Copilot suggests fields like Priority, Effort, Sprint, and Team.

steps.title: Generate a Sprint Planning Template
steps.instruction:
Now ask Copilot to create a reusable sprint planning checklist:

Create a sprint planning checklist I can use at the start of every sprint. Include steps for backlog grooming, capacity planning, goal setting, and team alignment. Format it as a markdown checklist.

This is something you can save and reuse every sprint.
steps.tip: Save prompts that produce useful outputs. Over time you will build a personal prompt library for recurring PM tasks.

steps.title: Panels Closed
steps.instruction:
We've closed the Settings tab and the sidebar for you.

For future reference:
• Settings: Ctrl+,
• Copilot Chat: Ctrl+Shift+I

steps.title: Explore Project Commands
steps.instruction:
We've opened the Command Palette for you. Type git to see version control commands available in VS Code.

As a PM, you do not need to memorize these commands — but knowing they exist helps you understand what your developers can do directly from their editor. The Command Palette is the universal search bar for VS Code capabilities.

steps.title: Design a Labeling Strategy
steps.instruction:
Consistent labels make filtering and reporting reliable. Ask Copilot:

Recommend a GitHub issue labeling strategy for a product development team. Include labels for issue type, priority, status, and area. For each label, suggest a name, color hex code, and one-sentence description.

Review the output and consider which labels would work for your team.

steps.title: Create a Milestone Plan
steps.instruction:
Ask Copilot to help structure your milestones:

I have a product release planned for Q3. Help me break this into monthly milestones with clear goals. The release includes a new dashboard feature, API performance improvements, and mobile app bug fixes.

Notice how Copilot organizes work into time-boxed milestones with specific deliverables.

steps.title: Panels Closed
steps.instruction:
We've closed the sidebar for you.

For future reference:
• Command Palette: Ctrl+Shift+P
• Copilot Chat: Ctrl+Shift+I

steps.title: Summarize a Pull Request
steps.instruction:
We have opened a new file for you. Paste the following sample PR description into it:

PR #247: Refactored the AuthService to use dependency injection. Removed static method calls in UserController and SessionManager. Added unit tests for token refresh flow. Updated the Docker compose file to include a Redis container for session caching.

Now select all the text, then ask Copilot Chat:

Summarize this pull request for a non-technical project manager. What changed, why does it matter, and is there any risk?

steps.title: Assess PR Scope and Impact
steps.instruction:
With the same text selected, ask a follow-up question:

How would you categorize this PR: bug fix, new feature, refactor, or infrastructure change? Should I flag this for extra testing?

This type of prompt helps you quickly triage PRs during your daily review without needing to understand the code.

steps.title: Generate a Standup Talking Point
steps.instruction:
Ask Copilot to turn the PR into something you can use in a meeting:

Write a one-sentence standup update about this PR that I can share with my team tomorrow morning.

This is a practical example of how Copilot saves you time every day.

steps.title: Panels Closed
steps.instruction:
We've closed the editor tab for you.

For future reference:
• New File: Ctrl+N

steps.title: Draft a Rough Issue
steps.instruction:
Clear your file and type a rough, informal issue description:

Users are complaining that the dashboard loads too slowly. We need to make it faster. Maybe add caching or something. Also the charts sometimes show stale data.

This is the kind of rough note a PM might jot down from a customer call.

steps.title: Improve the Issue with Copilot
steps.instruction:
Select all the text, then ask Copilot Chat:

Rewrite this as a well-structured GitHub issue. Include a clear title, context section, acceptance criteria, scope boundaries, and any questions that need answers before a developer can start.

Compare the before and after. Notice how Copilot adds structure, specificity, and testable criteria.

steps.title: Anticipate Developer Questions
steps.instruction:
Now ask Copilot:

What questions would a senior developer ask about this issue before starting work?

Review the questions. Each one represents a potential back-and-forth conversation you can preempt by adding the answers to the issue description upfront.
steps.tip: Running this prompt before you publish an issue can save a full day of clarification messages.

steps.title: Break It Down into Sub-Issues
steps.instruction:
Finally, ask Copilot to decompose the work:

Split this issue into 3-4 smaller, independently deliverable sub-issues. Each should be completable in one sprint or less. Format as a markdown list with a title and one-sentence description per sub-issue.

This is exactly what you would create as sub-issues linked to an epic in GitHub Issues.

steps.title: Panels Closed
steps.instruction:
We've closed the sidebar for you.

For future reference:
• Copilot Chat: Ctrl+Shift+I

steps.title: Prepare Sprint Data
steps.instruction:
Clear your file and paste this sample sprint data:

Sprint 14 (Jan 13-24): Completed: 18 issues, 47 story points Carried over: 3 issues (8 points) Bugs fixed: 5 Features shipped: Dashboard export, user notifications, API rate limiting Blockers resolved: 2 (CI pipeline fix, staging environment access) Outstanding: Mobile header alignment bug (blocked on design review)

This is the raw data a PM would collect at the end of a sprint.

steps.title: Generate a Sprint Report
steps.instruction:
Select all the text, then ask Copilot:

Write a sprint summary report from this data. Include sections for accomplishments, metrics, risks, and next sprint focus. Tone should be professional and concise.

Review the output. This is a report you could send to your engineering lead in minutes instead of hours.

steps.title: Adjust for an Executive Audience
steps.instruction:
Now ask Copilot to create a version for senior leadership:

Rewrite this sprint summary for a VP audience. Focus on business outcomes and progress toward quarterly goals. Remove technical details and keep it to one short paragraph.

Compare the two versions. Same data, different audiences, both generated in seconds.
steps.tip: Keep both prompt patterns saved. You can reuse them every sprint by swapping in new data.

steps.title: Browse Your Project Files
steps.instruction:
We've opened the Explorer sidebar. In a real project, this is where you would navigate to documentation, sprint reports, and configuration files created by your team.

Take a moment to see the files you have created during this lab. Each one is a reusable artifact you can adapt to your own projects.

steps.title: Analyze Project Health Data
steps.instruction:
Clear your file and paste this sample project health snapshot:

Current sprint issues: 22 total, 14 in progress, 3 blocked Aging issues (open 2+ sprints): #102 API migration, #89 Auth refactor, #134 Search redesign PRs awaiting review (3+ days): PR #201, PR #215, PR #218 Sprint carryover trend: 15%, 22%, 28% (last 3 sprints) Team member with no commits this sprint: Alex (out sick Mon-Wed)

steps.title: Surface Risks with Copilot
steps.instruction:
Select all the data, then ask Copilot:

Analyze this project health data and identify the top 3 risks. For each risk, explain why it matters and suggest a specific action I should take as the project manager.

Review the risk analysis. Notice how Copilot connects data points to actionable recommendations.

steps.title: Prepare Talking Points for Standup
steps.instruction:
Ask a follow-up:

Based on these risks, write 3 bullet points I should raise in tomorrow's standup. Keep each to one sentence.

These are ready-to-use talking points that address real problems instead of generic status updates.

steps.title: Panels Closed
steps.instruction:
We've closed the sidebar for you.

For future reference:
• Explorer: Ctrl+Shift+E
• Copilot Chat: Ctrl+Shift+I

steps.title: Draft a Risk Escalation Message
steps.instruction:
Using the risk analysis from the previous exercise, ask Copilot:

The sprint carryover trend is increasing (15% to 28% over 3 sprints). Draft a short message to my engineering manager explaining the trend, what I think is causing it, and my recommended next step. Tone should be collaborative, not accusatory.

Review the message. Notice how Copilot frames the concern constructively.

steps.title: Generate Retro Discussion Topics
steps.instruction:
Ask Copilot:

Based on the project health data I shared, suggest 3 discussion topics for our upcoming sprint retrospective. For each topic, include a framing question that invites team input rather than assigning blame.

Good retro topics come from data, not gut feelings. This prompt helps you bring evidence-based discussion items.
steps.tip: Framing questions as "How might we..." or "What would help us..." encourages problem-solving over finger-pointing.

steps.title: Try Copilot in the CLI
steps.instruction:
We've opened the Terminal for you. GitHub Copilot also works in the command line. Try typing:

gh copilot suggest "list merged PRs from last week"

Even as a PM, the CLI can help you quickly pull project data without navigating the GitHub web interface. This is especially useful for generating release summaries.

steps.title: Prepare Merged PR Data
steps.instruction:
Clear your file and paste this list of merged PRs:

Merged PRs for v2.4: - PR #230: Add CSV export to dashboard - PR #231: Fix timezone bug in scheduling module - PR #235: Upgrade authentication to OAuth 2.1 - PR #238: Improve search performance (3x faster queries) - PR #240: Add user notification preferences page - PR #242: Fix memory leak in background worker - PR #245: Update API docs for new endpoints

steps.title: Generate Release Notes
steps.instruction:
Select the PR list, then ask Copilot:

Write release notes for version 2.4 based on these merged PRs. Organize by category: Features, Bug Fixes, and Improvements. Write in user-facing language that focuses on benefits, not implementation details.

Review the output. These release notes are ready to share with customers.

steps.title: Create an Internal Version
steps.instruction:
Now ask for the engineering team's version:

Now write an internal version of these release notes for the engineering team. Include technical details, areas of the codebase affected, and any follow-up work needed.

Compare the two versions. Same source data, tailored for two different audiences.

steps.title: Panels Closed
steps.instruction:
We've closed the terminal and the sidebar for you.

For future reference:
• Terminal: Ctrl+`
• Copilot Chat: Ctrl+Shift+I

steps.title: Translate a Technical Update
steps.instruction:
Clear your file and paste this technical update from a developer:

We migrated the session store from in-memory to Redis, which required updating the Docker compose config and refactoring SessionManager to use async/await patterns. The auth token refresh flow now uses a sliding window expiration instead of fixed TTL. We also added circuit breaker logic to the external API calls in PaymentService to handle upstream timeouts gracefully.

Select the text and ask Copilot:

Rewrite this technical update for a VP who does not write code. Focus on what changed for users and the business. Keep it to 3-4 sentences.

steps.title: Verify Accuracy
steps.instruction:
Review both versions side by side. Ask Copilot:

Did the non-technical version preserve all the important facts from the original? List anything that was lost or oversimplified.

This verification step ensures you are not losing critical information when translating between audiences.
steps.tip: Always verify translations for accuracy. Copilot simplifies well, but occasionally it may omit a detail that matters to your stakeholders.

steps.title: Turn Metrics into a Narrative
steps.instruction:
Clear your file and paste these quarterly metrics:

Q3 Metrics: - Velocity: 42, 48, 51 story points (sprints 12-14) - Cycle time: reduced from 6.2 days to 4.1 days - Sprint completion rate: 78%, 82%, 88% - PR merge time: average 1.8 days (down from 3.2 days in Q2) - Bug-to-feature ratio: 30/70 (improved from 45/55 in Q2) - Features shipped: 12 (vs. 9 in Q2)

Select the data and ask Copilot:

Write a one-paragraph executive summary of these metrics for a quarterly business review. Lead with outcomes, show trends, and connect metrics to business impact.

steps.title: Add a Forward-Looking Statement
steps.instruction:
Ask Copilot:

Based on these Q3 trends, write a 2-3 sentence forecast paragraph for Q4. What should leadership expect if these trends continue? Include one specific recommendation.

This turns backward-looking data into forward-looking insight, which is what leadership values most from their PMs.

steps.title: Create a Slide-Ready Summary
steps.instruction:
Finally, ask Copilot:

Format these Q3 metrics as 4 bullet points for a presentation slide. Each bullet should have a bold metric name, the number, and a short phrase explaining why it matters. Keep each bullet to one line.

You now have three outputs from the same data: a narrative paragraph, a forecast, and a slide. All generated in minutes.

steps.title: Lab Complete!
steps.instruction:
Congratulations! 🎉 You have completed the PM Toolkit lab. You used Copilot Chat, the Terminal, Explorer, Settings, and the Command Palette to:

• Configure your workspace and discover VS Code project commands
• Design project board fields and sprint planning checklists
• Summarize pull requests for non-technical audiences
• Write well-structured GitHub issues with acceptance criteria
• Generate sprint reports tailored to different audiences
• Identify project risks and prepare actionable talking points
• Use the CLI to pull project data and draft release notes
• Turn raw metrics into leadership-ready narratives

Start building your personal prompt library with the prompts that worked best for you today.
steps.tip: Head back to the slides for key takeaways and your action plan, or close this panel to start applying these techniques to your real projects.
